**SOFTWARE LICENSING AGREEMENT**  
**THIS AGREEMENT** is made as of this [___] day of [___], 2025, by and between **TechNova Inc.**, a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business at 123 Innovation Drive, Silicon Valley, CA 94043, hereinafter referred to as **"Licensor"**, and **GlobalSystems Corp.**, a multinational corporation with its principal place of business at 456 Corporate Plaza, New York, NY 10001, hereinafter referred to as **"Licensee"**.  

**RECITALS**  

WHEREAS, Licensor is the sole owner of certain proprietary software, including but not limited to the software application known as "TechNova Core System" (hereinafter referred to as the "Software"), which is subject to intellectual property rights owned by Licensor and has been developed by Licensor in accordance with applicable laws and regulations;  
WHEREAS, Licensee desires to obtain a license to use the Software for specified purposes, and Licensor is willing to grant such a license on the terms and conditions set forth in this Agreement;  
WHEREAS, the parties agree that the Software shall be licensed under the terms and conditions set forth in this Agreement, and that the Software is subject to all applicable laws and regulations in the jurisdictions where it is used;  
WHEREAS, the parties acknowledge that the Software is a proprietary product and that all rights, title, and interest in and to the Software, including but not limited to all copyrights, patents, trademarks, trade secrets, and other intellectual property rights, shall remain the sole and exclusive property of Licensor, and the Licensee shall not acquire any ownership interest in the Software or any related intellectual property;  
WHEREAS, the parties further acknowledge that the licensing of the Software is subject to the terms and conditions set forth in this Agreement, and that the parties have entered into this Agreement in good faith and with the intention of creating a binding and enforceable agreement.  

**NOW, THEREFORE**, in consideration of the mutual covenants and promises contained herein, and for other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the parties agree as follows:  

**ARTICLE I – DEFINITIONS AND INTERPRETATION**  

**Section 1.1 – Definitions**  
For the purposes of this Agreement, the following terms shall have the meanings set forth below:  
(a) **"Agreement"** shall mean this Software Licensing Agreement, including all exhibits, schedules, and attachments hereto, and any amendments or supplements thereto, as may be entered into in accordance with the terms hereof.  
(b) **"Software"** shall mean the proprietary software application known as "TechNova Core System" and any related software, code, libraries, documentation, data, and other materials provided by Licensor to Licensee in connection with this Agreement.  
(c) **"Licensee"** shall mean GlobalSystems Corp., a multinational corporation, and its affiliates, subsidiaries, and successors-in-interest.  
(d) **"Licensor"** shall mean TechNova Inc., a Delaware corporation, and its affiliates, subsidiaries, and successors-in-interest.  
(e) **"License"** shall mean the non-exclusive, non-transferable, and non-sublicensable license granted by Licensor to Licensee under Article II of this Agreement to use the Software for the purposes set forth in Section 2.1 hereof.  
(f) **"Term"** shall mean the period from the Effective Date of this Agreement to the expiration date set forth in Section 10.2, unless terminated earlier in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.  
(g) **"Effective Date"** shall mean the date on which this Agreement is executed by both parties, or such later date as may be agreed upon by the parties in writing.  
(h) **"Confidential Information"** shall mean any and all information, whether written or oral, that is disclosed by one party to the other party in connection with this Agreement, including but not limited to technical specifications, business plans, marketing strategies, financial data, and other proprietary information, which is marked as confidential or is otherwise identified as confidential by the disclosing party.  
(i) **"Intellectual Property"** shall mean all copyrights, patents, trademarks, trade secrets, and other intellectual property rights, whether registered or unregistered, in and to the Software and all related materials.  
(j) **"Exclusivity"** shall mean the right granted by Licensor to Licensee to use the Software in the manner specified in this Agreement, and the parties acknowledge that such use is not exclusive in nature, unless otherwise agreed in writing.  
(k) **"Termination"** shall mean the termination of this Agreement by either party in accordance with the provisions set forth in Article X of this Agreement.  
(l) **"Payment"** shall mean the consideration paid by Licensee to Licensor in accordance with the terms set forth in Article III of this Agreement.  
(m) **"Compliance"** shall mean the adherence to all applicable laws, regulations, and contractual obligations in connection with the use of the Software.  
(n) **"Subsidiary"** shall mean any entity that is controlled, directly or indirectly, by Licensor or Licensee.  
(o) **"Affiliate"** shall mean any entity that controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with Licensor or Licensee.  
(p) **"Governing Law"** shall mean the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.  
 (q) **"Dispute"** shall mean any actual or potential controversy, claim, or dispute between the parties, whether arising from this Agreement, the performance of the Software, or any other matter related to the parties' relationship.  

**Section 1.2 – Interpretation**  
(a) The terms of this Agreement shall be interpreted in accordance with the ordinary meaning of the words used, and the parties agree that they have participated in the drafting of this Agreement and that the same shall be construed in a fair and equitable manner.  
(b) The headings of the sections and articles of this Agreement are for reference only and shall not be used to alter or modify the meaning of the text contained in the sections or articles.  
(c) All references in this Agreement to any article, section, or clause shall be deemed to refer to the corresponding article, section, or clause of this Agreement, unless otherwise specified.  
(d) In the event of a conflict between the terms of this Agreement and any other document, the terms of this Agreement shall prevail to the extent that they are not inconsistent with the terms of this Agreement.  
(e) The rights and obligations of the parties under this Agreement shall be interpreted in a manner consistent with the overall purpose and intent of the Agreement, and any ambiguity shall be resolved in favor of the parties’ mutual intent.  
(f) This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, as specified in Section 1.1(p), and shall be construed under the laws of such State.  
(g) All references to "including" or "including but not limited to" shall be deemed to be illustrative and not exhaustive.  
 (h) All provisions of this Agreement shall be deemed to be in pari materia, and shall be read together as a single, integrated document.  
(i) Any reference to "herein," "hereof," or "hereunder" shall be deemed to refer to this entire Agreement, including all exhibits, schedules, and attachments.  
(j) This Agreement may be executed in counterparts, each of which shall be deemed to be an original, and all counterparts shall be deemed to be one and the same instrument.  
(k) The parties agree that the language of this Agreement is intended to be binding, enforceable, and shall be construed in accordance with the principles of contract law.  
(l) The parties further agree that the terms of this Agreement shall not be amended, modified, or supplemented, except in writing signed by both parties.  
(m) The parties acknowledge that they have had the opportunity to review the terms of this Agreement, and that they are entering into this Agreement voluntarily, without coercion, duress, or undue influence.  

**ARTICLE II – GRANT OF LICENSE**  

**Section 2.1 – Scope of License**  
Licensor hereby grants to Licensee a non-exclusive, non-transferable, and non-sublicensable license to use the Software for internal business purposes, including but not limited to the development, testing, deployment, and operation of the Software within the internal infrastructure of Licensee’s organization. The license shall be limited to the specific Software as identified in Section 1.1(b), and shall not include any rights to use or modify the Software in any manner that would infringe upon the intellectual property rights of Licensor.  

**Section 2.2 – Exclusivity of License**  
(a) The license granted by Licensor to Licensee shall not be exclusive, and Licensor reserves the right to grant licenses to other parties, including but not limited to third-party developers, resellers, and end-users, for the same or similar Software.  
(b) Licensee acknowledges that the license granted hereunder does not grant any rights of exclusivity, and that Licensor may, at its sole discretion, enter into similar agreements with other parties.  
(c) Any rights to use the Software for purposes beyond those specified in Section 2.1 shall require the express written consent of Licensor, and shall be subject to additional terms and conditions.  

**Section 2.3 – Use of Software**  
(a) The Software shall be used solely for the purposes authorized by this Agreement and shall not be used in connection with any other business activity, including but not limited to commercial distribution, modification, or reverse engineering.  
(b) Licensee shall not, directly or indirectly, copy, adapt, translate, or otherwise alter the Software in any manner that would infringe upon the intellectual property rights of Licensor.  
(c) Licensee shall not permit any third party to use the Software without the prior written consent of Licensor.  
(d) Licensee shall not use the Software in any manner that violates any applicable laws, regulations, or policies, and shall ensure that the use of the Software is in compliance with all applicable legal requirements.  

**Section 2.4 – Restrictions on Use**  
(a) The Software shall not be used in connection with any activity that is illegal, unethical, or violates the terms of this Agreement.  
(b) The Software shall not be used for any purpose that involves the unauthorized collection, storage, or dissemination of personal data, or any other data that is protected by law.  
(c) The Software shall not be used in connection with any activity that involves the creation of derivative works, unless such derivative works are explicitly authorized by Licensor.  
(d) The Software shall not be used in connection with any activity that involves the provision of services to third parties, unless such services are explicitly authorized by Licensor.  
(e) The Software shall not be used in connection with any activity that involves the modification of the Software or the removal of any proprietary markings, watermarks, or notices.  
(f) The Software shall not be used in connection with any activity that involves the provision of the Software to third parties for use, unless such use is explicitly authorized by Licensor.  

**Section 2.5 – Compliance with Legal Requirements**  
(a) Licensee shall comply with all applicable laws, regulations, and policies in connection with the use of the Software, including but not limited to data protection laws, cybersecurity regulations, and intellectual property laws.  
(b) Licensee shall not use the Software in any manner that would cause Licensor to be liable to any third party, including but not limited to any claims related to the use of the Software.  
(c) Licensee shall ensure that the use of the Software does not violate any of the terms set forth in this Agreement, and shall be responsible for any and all legal consequences arising from the use of the Software.  

**Section 2.6 – Audit Rights**  
(a) Licensor shall have the right, at its sole discretion and upon reasonable notice, to audit Licensee’s use of the Software to ensure compliance with the terms of this Agreement.  

(b) Any audit conducted by Licensor shall be limited to the period of the Term and shall not include any data or information that is not relevant to the use of the Software.  
(c) Licensee shall cooperate fully with Licensor in any audit conducted under this Section and shall provide all necessary documentation and information upon request.  
(d) Any audit conducted by Licensor shall be limited to a maximum of two times per calendar year, and shall not be conducted more frequently than once every six months.  
(e) The cost of any audit shall be borne by Licensor, unless such audit is conducted at the request of Licensee, in which case Licensee shall bear the cost.  
(f) Licensee shall not restrict or interfere with any audit conducted by Licensor, and shall provide access to all relevant systems and data upon request.  

**Section 2.7 – Prohibited Uses**  
(a) The Software shall not be used in connection with any activity that is prohibited by law, including but not limited to activities involving the use of the Software for the unauthorized collection of personal data, the provision of services to third parties, or the creation of derivative works.  
  (b) The Software shall not be used in connection with any activity that involves the provision of the Software to third parties for use, unless such use is explicitly authorized by Licensor.  
(c) The Software shall not be used in connection with any activity that involves the modification of the Software or the removal of any proprietary markings, watermarks, or notices.  
(d) The Software shall not be used in connection with any activity that involves the creation of any derivative works, unless such derivative works are explicitly authorized by Licensor.  
(e) The Software shall not be used in connection with any activity that involves the use of the Software for purposes other than those specified in Section 2.1.  

**ARTICLE III – PAYMENT AND FEES**  

**Section 3.1 – Licensing Fees**  
(a) In consideration of the license granted by Licensor to Licensee under this Agreement, Licensee shall pay to Licensor the following fees:  
(i) A one-time licensing fee of Two Hundred Thousand Dollars ($200,000.00) for the initial grant of the license.  
(ii) A recurring monthly maintenance fee of Fifteen Thousand Dollars ($15,000.00), payable on the first day of each calendar month during the Term of this Agreement.  
(iii) A one-time implementation fee of Fifty Thousand Dollars ($50,000.00), payable upon execution of this Agreement.  
(b) The fees set forth in this Section shall be paid in United States Dollars (USD) and shall be due and payable in accordance with the payment terms set forth in Section 3.2.  
(c) Any additional fees or charges may be imposed by Licensor at its sole discretion, provided that such fees are reasonable and necessary for the provision of the Software or the performance of the services associated therewith.  

**Section 3.2 – Payment Terms**  
(a) The initial licensing fee, implementation fee, and any other fees required under this Agreement shall be paid by Licensee to Licensor in accordance with the following payment schedule:  
(i) The initial licensing fee of Two Hundred Thousand Dollars ($200,000.00) shall be paid in full on the Effective Date of this Agreement.  
(ii) The implementation fee of Fifty Thousand Dollars ($50,000.00) shall be paid in full on the Effective Date of this Agreement.  
(iii) The recurring monthly maintenance fee of Fifteen Thousand Dollars ($15,000.00) shall be paid on the first day of each calendar month during the Term of this Agreement.  
(b) All payments shall be made by wire transfer to the bank account specified by Licensor in writing.  
(c) Licensee shall not be entitled to any refunds or credits for any fees paid under this Agreement unless such fees are subject to a specific refund policy, which shall be set forth in writing by Licensor.  
(d) If any payment is not made within thirty (30) days of the due date, Licensor shall have the right to charge interest on the overdue amount at a rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, compounded monthly, until such payment is made in full.  
(e) Licensee shall be responsible for all taxes, fees, and other charges associated with the payment of the fees under this Agreement, including but not limited to sales tax, value-added tax, and any other applicable taxes.  

**Section 3.3 – Payment Method**  
(a) Licensee shall pay all fees under this Agreement by wire transfer to the account specified by Licensor in writing.  
(b) Licensee shall ensure that all payments are made in a timely manner and that all required documentation is provided to Licensor to facilitate the processing of such payments.  
(c) Licensor shall provide Licensee with a receipt or confirmation of payment upon receipt of such payment.  
(d) Licensee shall not be entitled to any discounts, credits, or other reductions in fees unless such reductions are explicitly stated in writing by Licensor.  


**Section 3.4 – Late Payments**  
(a) If any payment is not made within thirty (30) days of the due date, Licensor shall have the right to charge interest on the overdue amount at a rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, compounded monthly, until such payment is made in full.  
(b) In the event that Licensee fails to make any payment within thirty (30) days of the due date, Licensor may, at its sole discretion, suspend the provision of the Software or any related services until such payment is made in full.  
(c) Licensee acknowledges that the failure to make any payment on time may result in the termination of this Agreement, and that such termination shall be in accordance with the provisions of Article X of this Agreement.  

**Section 3.5 – Payment Disputes**  
(a) In the event of a dispute regarding the amount or timing of any payment, the parties shall resolve such dispute in good faith and in accordance with the procedures set forth in Section 15.1 of this Agreement.  
(b) If the dispute cannot be resolved through negotiation, the parties agree that the dispute shall be resolved through the arbitration procedures set forth in Section 15.1 of this Agreement.  
(c) The parties acknowledge that the payment of fees is a material obligation under this Agreement and that failure to make such payments may result in the termination of this Agreement, as provided in Section 10.1 of this Agreement.  

**Section 3.6 – Invoicing**  
(a) Licensor shall issue an invoice to Licensee for all fees due under this Agreement, which shall include a breakdown of the fees and the due date for payment.  
(b) Licensor shall provide Licensee with an invoice for each payment due, and Licensee shall have the right to review and question any items included in such invoice.  
(c) If Licensee has any questions or disputes regarding an invoice, it shall notify Licensor in writing within ten (10) business days of receipt of the invoice.  
(d) If Licensee disputes any item on an invoice, Licensor shall have the right to investigate such dispute and may require Licensee to provide additional information or documentation to support its position.  
(e) If the dispute cannot be resolved within thirty (30) days of the date of the invoice, Licensee shall be deemed to have accepted the invoice and the fees shall be due and payable in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.  

**Section 3.7 – Refunds**  
(a) Licensor shall not be obligated to provide any refunds for any fees paid under this Agreement unless such fees are subject to a specific refund policy, which shall be set forth in writing by Licensor.  
(b) If a refund is required by law, Licensor shall provide such refund in accordance with the applicable legal requirements.  
(c) Licensee shall not be entitled to any refunds for any fees paid under this Agreement unless such refunds are explicitly stated in writing by Licensor.  
(d) In the event of a refund, Licensor shall issue a refund to Licensee in accordance with the payment method used to make the original payment.  
(e) Refunds shall be issued within thirty (30) days of the request for such a refund, unless the refund is subject to a specific processing time, which shall be set forth in writing by Licensor.  

**Section 3.8 – Taxes**  
(a) Licensee shall be responsible for all taxes, duties, fees, and other charges associated with the payment of fees under this Agreement, including but not limited to sales tax, value-added tax, and any other applicable taxes.  
(b) If any taxes are imposed on the fees paid under this Agreement, Licensee shall pay such taxes in accordance with the applicable legal requirements.  
(c) Licensor shall not be liable for any taxes, duties, fees, or other charges that may be imposed on Licensee in connection with the fees paid under this Agreement.  
(d) Licensee acknowledges that it is responsible for all tax obligations arising from the payment of fees under this Agreement.  

**Section 3.9 – Payment Schedule**  
(a) The payment schedule for all fees under this Agreement shall be as follows:  
(i) Initial Licensing Fee: Two Hundred Thousand Dollars ($200,000.00), payable on the Effective Date of this Agreement.  
(ii) Implementation Fee: Fifty Thousand Dollars ($50,000.00), payable on the Effective Date of this Agreement.  
(iii) Monthly Maintenance Fee: Fifteen Thousand Dollars ($15,000.00), payable on the first day of each calendar month during the Term of this Agreement.  
(b) All payments shall be made in United States Dollars (USD) and shall be due and payable in accordance with the payment terms set forth in Section 3.2.  
(c) Any additional fees or charges may be imposed by Licensor at its sole discretion, provided that such fees are reasonable and necessary for the provision of the Software or the performance of the services associated therewith.  

(d) The payment schedule shall be subject to change by Licensor in its sole discretion, provided that such changes are communicated to Licensee in writing.  

**Section 3.10 – Late Charges**  
(a) If any payment is not made within thirty (30) days of the due date, Licensor shall have the right to charge interest on the overdue amount at a rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, compounded monthly, until such payment is made in full.  
(b) In the event that Licensee fails to make any payment within thirty (30) days of the due date, Licensor may, at its sole discretion, suspend the provision of the Software or any related services until such payment is made in full.  
(c) Licensee acknowledges that the failure to make any payment on time may result in the termination of this Agreement, and that such termination shall be in accordance with the provisions of Article X of this Agreement.  

**Section 3.11 – Currency and Exchange Rate**  
(a) All payments under this Agreement shall be made in United States Dollars (USD), unless otherwise agreed in writing by the parties.  
(b) If any payment is made in a different currency, such currency shall be converted to USD using the exchange rate of the bank at the time of payment.  
(c) Licensee shall bear the risk of any fluctuations in the exchange rate and shall be responsible for any currency conversion charges.  
   (d) Licensor shall not be liable for any losses, costs, or expenses arising from currency conversion or exchange rate fluctuations.  

**Section 3.12 – Payment Records**  

(a) Licensor shall maintain accurate records of all payments received under this Agreement.  
(b) Licensee shall provide Licensor with a copy of all payment records upon request.  
(c) Licensor shall have the right to audit Licensee’s payment records to ensure compliance with the terms of this Agreement.  
(d) Any audit conducted by Licensor shall be limited to the period of the Term and shall not include any data or information that is not relevant to the use of the Software.  
(e) Licensee shall cooperate fully with Licensor in any audit conducted under this Section and shall provide all necessary documentation and information upon request.  
(f) The cost of any audit shall be borne by Licensor, unless such audit is conducted at the request of Licensee, in which case Licensee shall bear the cost.  
(g) Licensee shall not restrict or interfere with any audit conducted by Licensor, and shall provide access to all relevant systems and data upon request.  

**Section 3.13 – Currency Conversion**  
(a) If any payment is made in a different currency, such currency shall be converted to USD using the exchange rate of the bank at the time of payment.  
(b) Licensee shall bear the risk of any fluctuations in the exchange rate and shall be responsible for any currency conversion charges.  
(c) Licensor shall not be liable for any losses, costs, or expenses arising from currency conversion or exchange rate fluctuations.  
(d) Licensor shall have the right to adjust the payment amounts based on the exchange rate at the time of payment.  
(e) Any adjustments to the payment amounts shall be communicated to Licensee in writing.  
(f) Licensee shall have the right to dispute any adjustments to the payment amounts, provided that such dispute is made in writing within ten (10) business days of receipt of the adjustment notice.  
(g) If Licensee disputes any adjustment to the payment amounts, Licensor shall have the right to investigate such dispute and may require Licensee to provide additional information or documentation to support its position.  
(h) If the dispute cannot be resolved within thirty (30) days of the date of the adjustment notice, Licensee shall be deemed to have accepted the adjustment and the payment amounts shall be due and payable in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.

Such measures shall include, but are not limited to, encryption, access controls, regular security audits, and employee training on data protection.

scenario_title: Motion to Dismiss for Lack of Jurisdiction
document:
**IN THE UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT**  
**FOR THE DISTRICT OF COLUMBIA**  

**GREENFIELD ENTERPRISES, INC.,**  
Plaintiff,  
v.  
**BLUEWAVE INDUSTRIES, LLC,**  
Defendant.  

**Case No.: 24-cv-00123**  

**MOTION TO DISMISS FOR LACK OF JURISDICTION**  

This Motion to Dismiss for Lack of Jurisdiction is filed by the Defendant, BlueWave Industries, LLC, pursuant to the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, specifically Rule 12(b)(1), and is based upon the absence of subject matter jurisdiction over the claims asserted by the Plaintiff, Greenfield Enterprises, Inc. The Defendant respectfully moves this Court to dismiss the entire complaint for lack of jurisdiction, both on the grounds of complete absence of jurisdiction and the failure of the Plaintiff to establish a jurisdictional basis that meets the requirements of 28 U.S.C. § 1331, 28 U.S.C. § 1332, or any other applicable federal statute.  

---

### I. INTRODUCTION

This motion arises from the Complaint filed by Greenfield Enterprises, Inc., a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business in the State of California, against BlueWave Industries, LLC, a Nevada corporation with its principal place of business in the State of Texas. The Complaint alleges a variety of claims, including breach of contract, tortious interference with business relations, and unjust enrichment, all of which are rooted in state law. The Plaintiff, however, has attempted to assert jurisdiction over the Defendant in this federal court by invoking the general federal question jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1331 and by claiming that the amount in controversy exceeds the jurisdictional threshold of $75,000.  

The Defendant, however, contends that neither of these bases for federal jurisdiction is valid. First, the claims asserted by the Plaintiff are not based upon a federal question, but rather are grounded in the laws of the State of Texas, which governs the parties’ contractual and tortious relationships. Second, the amount in controversy, as alleged, does not meet the jurisdictional threshold required under 28 U.S.C. § 1331. Moreover, the Plaintiff has failed to allege that the matter involves a substantial federal question that justifies federal jurisdiction.  


The Defendant further argues that the Complaint fails to establish the necessary diversity of citizenship required for diversity jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1332. Specifically, the Plaintiff has not alleged that the Defendant is a citizen of a state other than California, and the Defendant is a Nevada corporation with its principal place of business in Texas, which may not be sufficient to establish complete diversity.  

The Defendant is therefore compelled to move this Court to dismiss the entire Complaint for lack of jurisdiction, as the Court lacks the authority to adjudicate the claims asserted by the Plaintiff.  

---

### II. FACTUAL AND PROCEDURAL BACKGROUND

The parties are corporate entities operating in the technology and energy sectors, with Greenfield Enterprises, Inc., being a California-based company that has engaged in a series of business dealings with BlueWave Industries, LLC, a Nevada-based entity. The relationship between the two companies has been ongoing for several years, with BlueWave Industries, LLC, providing certain services to Greenfield Enterprises, Inc., under a series of contractual agreements.  

The Plaintiff’s Complaint alleges that the Defendant has breached the terms of these agreements and has engaged in tortious conduct that has caused the Plaintiff substantial financial loss. The Complaint also claims that the Defendant has interfered with the Plaintiff’s business relations and has unjustly enriched itself at the Plaintiff’s expense.  

The Plaintiff, in its Complaint, asserts that the federal court has jurisdiction over the case because the claims are based upon a federal question. The Plaintiff also alleges that the amount in controversy exceeds the jurisdictional threshold under 28 U.S.C. § 1331. The Plaintiff further claims that the Defendant is a citizen of a state other than California, thereby satisfying the diversity requirements of 28 U.S.C. § 1332.  

The Defendant, however, contends that the allegations in the Complaint are entirely based upon state law and that the federal court lacks subject matter jurisdiction. The Defendant also contends that the amount in controversy is not sufficiently established to justify federal jurisdiction. Moreover, the Defendant asserts that the Plaintiff has failed to establish that the Defendant is a citizen of a state other than California, thereby failing to meet the requirements for diversity jurisdiction.  

---

### III. LEGAL STANDARD

Under Rule 12(b)(1) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, a defendant may move to dismiss a complaint for lack of subject matter jurisdiction. The burden of establishing jurisdiction rests with the plaintiff, and the court may examine the pleadings, affidavits, and other evidence to determine whether jurisdiction exists.  

The standard for determining jurisdiction is strict and requires the plaintiff to demonstrate not only that the court has the power to adjudicate the case, but also that the case falls within the scope of that power. The court may consider the allegations in the complaint, but may also look beyond the pleadings to determine whether jurisdiction exists.  

In the case of a motion to dismiss for lack of jurisdiction, the court may not consider the truth of the allegations but may consider the legal sufficiency of the jurisdictional basis. The motion may also be granted if the court finds that the jurisdictional requirements have not been met.  

In the absence of a valid basis for jurisdiction, the court may dismiss the case with prejudice or without prejudice, depending on the circumstances.  

---

### IV. ARGUMENT


#### A. The Claims in the Complaint Are Grounded in State Law and Are Not Based Upon a Federal Question

The Plaintiff’s Complaint is entirely based upon the laws of the State of Texas, which governs the contractual and tortious relationships between the parties. The claims of breach of contract, tortious interference with business relations, and unjust enrichment are all state law claims that do not involve any federal question.  

Under 28 U.S.C. § 1331, federal courts have jurisdiction over civil actions arising under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States. However, this jurisdiction is limited to cases that are based upon federal law or that involve a substantial federal question. The claims asserted by the Plaintiff are not based upon any federal law, nor do they involve a substantial federal question.  

The Plaintiff’s argument that the claims are based upon a federal question is therefore baseless. The claims are purely state law claims that do not require the application of federal law. Therefore, the federal court lacks subject matter jurisdiction over the case.  

#### B. The Amount in Controversy Does Not Meet the Jurisdictional Threshold Under 28 U.S.C. § 1331

The Plaintiff also asserts that the amount in controversy exceeds the jurisdictional threshold of $75,000 under 28 U.S.C. § 1331. However, the Complaint does not provide any specific figures or evidence to support this assertion. The Plaintiff has merely alleged that the amount in controversy is substantial and that it exceeds the jurisdictional threshold.  

Under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, the plaintiff must provide a specific amount in controversy to support a claim under 28 U.S.C. § 1331. The Court may not assume that the amount in controversy is satisfied merely by the plaintiff’s allegations. The burden is on the plaintiff to demonstrate that the amount in controversy meets the jurisdictional threshold.  

In the absence of specific allegations or evidence that the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000, the court may not find that the jurisdictional threshold has been met. Therefore, the federal court lacks subject matter jurisdiction over the case.  

#### C. The Plaintiff Has Failed to Establish Diversity of Citizenship Required for Diversity Jurisdiction

The Plaintiff also asserts that the case is subject to diversity jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1332. However, the Complaint does not provide sufficient information to establish that the parties are citizens of different states.  

The Court must determine whether the parties are citizens of different states, and if so, whether the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000. The Plaintiff has failed to allege that the Defendant is a citizen of a state other than California, and the Defendant is a Nevada corporation with its principal place of business in Texas. Therefore, the Plaintiff has not satisfied the diversity requirement.  

The Court may not infer that the Defendant is a citizen of a state other than California merely because the Defendant is a Nevada corporation. The Court must determine the actual citizenship of the Defendant based on the allegations in the Complaint and any supporting evidence.  

#### D. The Court Lacks Jurisdiction to Adjudicate the Claims

In conclusion, the Court lacks subject matter jurisdiction over the claims asserted by the Plaintiff. The claims are based upon state law and do not involve any federal question. The amount in controversy has not been established, and the Plaintiff has failed to demonstrate that the parties are citizens of different states. Therefore, the federal court lacks jurisdiction over the case.  

The Defendant respectfully moves this Court to dismiss the entire Complaint for lack of jurisdiction, and to dismiss the case with prejudice.  

---

### V. CONCLUSION

For the reasons set forth above, the Defendant respectfully requests that this Motion to Dismiss for Lack of Jurisdiction be granted, and that the entire Complaint be dismissed from this action. The Defendant further requests that the Court award it costs and attorneys’ fees incurred in connection with this motion, pursuant to 28 U.S.C. § 1445 or any other applicable statute.  

Respectfully submitted,  

**BLUEWAVE INDUSTRIES, LLC**  
By: /s/ Jane Doe  
Jane Doe, Esq.  
Attorney for Defendant  

**Dated:** April 12, 2025  

---  

**CERTIFICATE OF SERVICE**

I, Jane Doe, Esq., hereby certify that a true and correct copy of this Motion to Dismiss for Lack of Jurisdiction was served upon the Plaintiff, Greenfield Enterprises, Inc., on April 12, 2025, by electronic mail and first-class United States mail.  

**Respectfully submitted,**

**Jane Doe, Esq.**  
Attorney for Defendant  

**Dated:** April 12, 2025  

---  

**APPENDIX A: RELEVANT LEGAL DOCUMENTS AND REFERENCES**

**Article I: Jurisdictional Requirements**  
**Section 1.1: Federal Question Jurisdiction**  
Under 28 U.S.C. § 1331, federal courts have jurisdiction over civil actions arising under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States. However, this jurisdiction is limited to cases that are based upon federal law or that involve a substantial federal question.  

**Section 1.2: Diversity Jurisdiction**  
Under 28 U.S.C. § 1332, federal courts have jurisdiction over civil actions between citizens of different states, provided that the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000. The Court must determine whether the parties are citizens of different states, and if so, whether the amount in controversy meets the jurisdictional threshold.  

**Section 1.3: Amount in Controversy**  
Under 28 U.S.C. § 1331, the plaintiff must demonstrate that the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000. The Court may not assume that the amount in controversy is satisfied merely by the plaintiff’s allegations. The burden is on the plaintiff to demonstrate that the amount in controversy meets the jurisdictional threshold.  

**Section 1.4: Citizenship of Parties**  

Under 28 U.S.C. § 1332, the Court must determine the citizenship of the parties based on their principal place of business. The Plaintiff has failed to allege that the Defendant is a citizen of a state other than California. Therefore, the Court lacks diversity jurisdiction.  

**Section 1.5: Federal Question Jurisdiction**  
The claims asserted by the Plaintiff are entirely based upon state law. Therefore, the federal court lacks subject matter jurisdiction over the case.  

---  

**APPENDIX B: LEGAL CLAUSES AND DEFINITIONS**

**Clause 2.1: Definition of "Federal Question"**  
A federal question is a claim that is based upon the laws, treaties, or Constitution of the United States.  

**Clause 2.2: Definition of "Diversity of Citizenship"**  
Diversity of citizenship is a requirement for diversity jurisdiction, which requires that the parties be citizens of different states.  

**Clause 2.3: Definition of "Amount in Controversy"**  
The amount in controversy is the total value of the claim that is subject to litigation. The plaintiff must demonstrate that the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000.  

**Clause 2.4: Definition of "Citizenship"**

scenario_title: Motion to Dismiss for Lack of Jurisdiction
document:
**UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT**  
**FOR THE DISTRICT OF COLUMBIA**  
**GREENFIELD ENTERPRISES, INC.,**  
Plaintiff,  
**v.**  
**BLUEWAVE INDUSTRIES, LLC,**  
Defendant.  

**Case No.: 24-cv-001234**  
**Caption:** Motion to Dismiss for Lack of Jurisdiction  

**MOTION TO DISMISS FOR LACK OF JURISDICTION**  

**Plaintiff, Greenfield Enterprises, Inc.,** by and through undersigned counsel, hereby moves this Court for an order dismissing this action, and all claims asserted in the Complaint for want of subject matter jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1331 and 28 U.S.C. § 1332, and for any other relief this Court deems just and proper.  

---

**I. INTRODUCTION**  

This motion is brought by the Defendant, BlueWave Industries, LLC, in response to the Complaint filed by the Plaintiff, Greenfield Enterprises, Inc., which seeks to assert jurisdiction in the United States District Court for the District of Columbia. The Complaint, as set forth in its entirety, is predicated upon a claim that the Defendant has engaged in various wrongful acts and omissions in the course of its business dealings with the Plaintiff, and that these acts have occurred in a manner sufficient to establish federal jurisdiction under the diversity jurisdiction provisions of 28 U.S.C. § 1332.  

However, upon a close and thorough review of the allegations and the facts set forth in the Complaint, it becomes abundantly clear that the Court lacks the necessary jurisdiction to entertain this action. The assertion of jurisdiction by the Plaintiff is not only legally unsound but is also factually unsupported. The Court’s jurisdiction is derived from two primary sources: (1) the federal question jurisdiction granted by 28 U.S.C. § 1331, and (2) the diversity jurisdiction granted by 28 U.S.C. § 1332. Neither of these sources is applicable to the claims in this action.  

The Complaint does not, in fact, present any federal question that is within the purview of this Court. Instead, the claims asserted by the Plaintiff are rooted entirely in state law, and thus, the Court does not have the authority to entertain them under the federal question jurisdiction. Furthermore, the diversity jurisdiction is also not applicable, as the parties are not of diverse citizenship, and the amount in controversy does not satisfy the statutory threshold.  

Therefore, this Court is without jurisdiction over this action, and accordingly, the entire Complaint must be dismissed, and the case must be remanded to the appropriate state court.  

---

**II. FACTUAL BACKGROUND**  

The factual allegations in the Complaint, as presented by the Plaintiff, are as follows:  

Greenfield Enterprises, Inc., a company headquartered in the State of Georgia, is engaged in the manufacturing and distribution of industrial equipment and machinery. BlueWave Industries, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company, is a company based in the State of Texas. According to the Complaint, BlueWave Industries, LLC, has engaged in various acts of misconduct, including breach of contract, fraudulent misrepresentation, and deceptive trade practices, in its dealings with Greenfield Enterprises, Inc.  

The Complaint asserts that these acts have occurred primarily in the State of Texas, and that as a result, the Court of the United States for the District of Columbia has jurisdiction over the matter under the doctrine of forum selection clauses, or alternatively, under the diversity jurisdiction. The Plaintiff further contends that the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000, and that the parties are of diverse citizenship.  

However, a review of the allegations in the Complaint reveals that the Plaintiff has failed to establish either a federal question or diversity jurisdiction. The claims are based entirely on state law, and the parties are not of diverse citizenship. Moreover, the allegations do not indicate that the acts giving rise to the claims occurred in the District of Columbia, nor that the Court has any connection to the subject matter of the litigation.  

Furthermore, the Complaint does not contain any mention of any federal statute, regulation, or constitutional provision upon which the Plaintiff seeks to base its claims. The claims are all grounded in state law, and therefore, the federal question jurisdiction does not apply.  

As for diversity jurisdiction, the Plaintiff has not demonstrated that the parties are of diverse citizenship. The Plaintiff is a Georgia corporation, and the Defendant is a Delaware limited liability company with its principal place of business in Texas. Therefore, the citizenship of both parties is based on their respective states of incorporation, and they are not of diverse citizenship.  

Moreover, the amount in controversy is not clearly established. While the Plaintiff states that the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000, it does not provide any supporting evidence, and the allegations do not sufficiently indicate the actual value of the claims. Therefore, the diversity jurisdiction is not satisfied.  

---

**III. LEGAL STANDARD**  

A motion to dismiss for lack of jurisdiction is a matter of law and must be decided by the Court upon a review of the pleadings and any supporting materials. Under Rule 12(b)(1) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, a party may move to dismiss an action for lack of subject matter jurisdiction.  

The burden of proving the existence of jurisdiction rests upon the plaintiff, and the plaintiff must demonstrate that jurisdiction exists in order for the action to proceed. If the Court finds that the plaintiff has failed to establish jurisdiction, then the entire action must be dismissed, and the case must be remanded to the appropriate state court.  

The determination of subject matter jurisdiction is a question of law, and the Court must examine the pleadings to determine whether the plaintiff has presented a claim that falls within the Court’s jurisdiction. If the pleadings fail to demonstrate that the Court has jurisdiction, the Court must dismiss the action.  

In addition to the burden of proof, the Court may also consider the adequacy of the plaintiff’s allegations. If the plaintiff’s claims are based on state law, then the Court does not have jurisdiction unless the claim is grounded in a federal question or the parties are of diverse citizenship. If the plaintiff has not demonstrated that the Court has jurisdiction, then the Court must dismiss the action.  

---

**IV. ARGUMENT**  

**A. Lack of Federal Question Jurisdiction**  

The first and most fundamental issue in this case is whether the Court has federal question jurisdiction. Under 28 U.S.C. § 1331, federal question jurisdiction exists when the claim is based on a federal statute, regulation, or constitutional provision.  

In the present case, the Plaintiff has failed to assert any claim that is based on federal law. All of the claims set forth in the Complaint are based on state law, and none of the allegations relate to any federal statute, regulation, or constitutional provision.  

The allegations in the Complaint are entirely state law-based. The Plaintiff asserts claims for breach of contract, fraudulent misrepresentation, and deceptive trade practices, all of which are governed by the laws of the State of Texas and the State of Georgia. These claims do not involve any federal statutes, regulations, or constitutional provisions. Therefore, the federal question jurisdiction does not apply.  

Furthermore, the Plaintiff has not alleged any facts that would establish that the Court has federal question jurisdiction. The allegations are entirely based on state law, and the Court has no jurisdiction over claims that are grounded in state law unless the claim is brought under a federal statute or the parties are of diverse citizenship.  

Therefore, the Court does not have federal question jurisdiction, and the entire action must be dismissed for lack of jurisdiction.  

**B.  Lack of Diversity Jurisdiction**  

The second issue in this case is whether the Court has diversity jurisdiction. Under 28 U.S.C. § 1332, diversity jurisdiction exists when the parties are citizens of different states and the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000.  

The Plaintiff is a Georgia corporation, and the Defendant is a Delaware limited liability company with its principal place of business in Texas. Therefore, the parties are not of diverse citizenship. The Plaintiff is a Georgia corporation, and the Defendant is a Delaware company, and thus, they are not citizens of different states.  

Furthermore, the amount in controversy is not clearly established. While the Plaintiff states that the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000, it does not provide any supporting evidence. The allegations in the Complaint do not clearly indicate the actual value of the claims. Therefore, the diversity jurisdiction is not satisfied.  

Moreover, the Court must also consider whether the parties are actually citizens of the states they claim to be citizens of. In determining citizenship for the purposes of diversity jurisdiction, the Court must look to the state of incorporation and the principal place of business.  

The Plaintiff is a Georgia corporation, and the Defendant is a Delaware company with its principal place of business in Texas. Therefore, the parties are not citizens of different states. The Defendant is a Delaware company, and thus, its citizenship is based on its state of incorporation, not its principal place of business.  

Therefore, the parties are not of diverse citizenship, and the diversity jurisdiction is not applicable.  

**C. Forum Selection Clauses and Venue**  

The Plaintiff has also attempted to assert jurisdiction on the grounds of forum selection clauses and venue. However, the Court must consider whether the forum selection clause is valid and whether the Plaintiff has demonstrated that the Court has jurisdiction under such clauses.  

The Complaint does not mention any forum selection clause. Therefore, the Court must conclude that the Plaintiff has failed to assert any such clause as a basis for jurisdiction.  

Furthermore, the Court must also consider whether the Plaintiff has established that the forum selection clause is valid. The validity of a forum selection clause depends on whether it is enforceable under the law of the state where the contract was formed and whether it is enforceable under the law of the forum.  

The Plaintiff has not provided any evidence that a forum selection clause exists, nor has it provided any evidence that such a clause is valid. Therefore, the Court must conclude that the forum selection clause is not applicable to this case.  

Additionally, the Court must consider whether the venue is appropriate. The venue is determined based on the location of the defendant’s principal place of business or where the contract was executed. However, the Complaint does not indicate that the defendant’s principal place of business is in the District of Columbia. Therefore, the venue is not appropriate, and the Court does not have jurisdiction based on venue.  

Therefore, the Court does not have jurisdiction based on forum selection clauses or venue.  

---

**V. CONCLUSION**  

In conclusion, the Court has reviewed the allegations and the facts set forth in the Complaint and has determined that the Court lacks jurisdiction over this action. The claims asserted by the Plaintiff are entirely based on state law, and the parties are not of diverse citizenship. Additionally, the amount in controversy is not clearly established, and the forum selection clause is not applicable.  

Therefore, the Court must dismiss this action for lack of jurisdiction. The entire action must be dismissed, and the case must be remanded to the appropriate state court.  

**WHEREFORE, Defendant, BlueWave Industries, LLC, respectfully requests that this Court:**

1. Dismiss the entire action with prejudice for lack of subject matter jurisdiction;  
2. Remand the case to the appropriate state court for further proceedings;  
3. Award Defendant its costs and disbursements incurred in connection with this motion;  
4. Grant any other relief this Court deems just and proper.  

Respectfully submitted,

**APPENDIX C: TABLE OF DEFINITIONS**  

- **Jurisdiction:** The legal authority of a court to hear and decide a case.  
- **Subject Matter Jurisdiction:** The authority of a court to adjudicate a particular type of case or issue.  
- **Diversity Jurisdiction:** The authority of a federal court to hear a case in which the parties are citizens of different states.  
- **Federal Question Jurisdiction:** The authority of a federal court to hear a case that involves a question of federal law.  
- **Forum Selection Clause:** A contractual provision that designates a particular forum for the resolution of disputes.

**IN THE SUPERIOR COURT OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA**  
**FOR THE COUNTY OF LOS ANGELES**  

**CASE NO.: 23-XXXXXXX**  

**SARAH MITCHELL,**  
Plaintiff,  
v.  
**ECOSAFE MANUFACTURING, INC.,**  
Defendant.  

**COMPLAINT FOR NEGLIGENCE AND STRICT LIABILITY IN PRODUCT LIABILITY**  

**I. INTRODUCTION**  

This Complaint is filed by Plaintiff, Sarah Mitchell, against Defendant, EcoSafe Manufacturing, Inc., a California corporation, for injuries sustained as a result of the defective and unreasonably dangerous product manufactured and sold by Defendant. This action arises under the principles of product liability law as recognized and applied within the jurisdiction of California, including but not limited to the California Civil Code, California Business and Professions Code, and the common law of torts. The allegations set forth in this Complaint are based upon the facts, circumstances, and legal obligations of the parties, and they are intended to provide a full and complete statement of the cause of action against Defendant.  

The allegations contained in this Complaint are not limited to the injuries and damages suffered by Plaintiff, but extend to the potential for further harm and the broader implications of the defective nature of the product. This action is brought in accordance with the jurisdiction and venue of the Superior Court of the State of California, County of Los Angeles, where the events giving rise to the claims occurred, and where the Defendant has its principal place of business.  

**II. PARTIES**  

A. **Plaintiff:**  
Sarah Mitchell is a natural person, a resident of Los Angeles, California, and a resident of the County of Los Angeles. At the time of the incidents giving rise to this action, she was of the age of majority in the State of California and possessed full legal capacity to enter into legal contracts, to sue and be sued, and to bring this action.  

B. **Defendant:**      
EcoSafe Manufacturing, Inc., is a corporation organized and existing under the laws of the State of California, with its principal place of business located at 123 Greenway Drive, Riverside, California 92507. Defendant is engaged in the business of manufacturing, distributing, and selling household cleaning products, including a line of biodegradable and eco-friendly cleaning agents, including the product known as “EcoClean Pro.”  

**III. JURISDICTION AND VENUE**  


This Court has jurisdiction over the subject matter of this action and over the parties hereto under California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.10 and § 410.20, which provide for the jurisdiction of the Superior Court of California over civil actions involving tort claims, including those arising from defective products.  

Venue is properly laid in the Superior Court of the County of Los Angeles because: (a) the incident giving rise to this action occurred within the County of Los Angeles; (b) the Defendant maintains its principal place of business within the County of Los Angeles; (c) the injuries and damages sustained by the Plaintiff occurred in the County of Los Angeles; and (d) the Defendant is a resident of the County of Los Angeles.  

**IV. FACTUAL BACKGROUND**  

A. **The Product in Question**  
The product at the center of this litigation is a line of biodegradable and eco-friendly cleaning agents sold under the brand name “EcoClean Pro.” These products were marketed and sold by Defendant as environmentally safe, non-toxic, and suitable for use in households, including kitchens and bathrooms. The product in question, specifically, was a concentrated version of the “EcoClean Pro” line, which was labeled as a “multi-surface cleaner” and was distributed in 16-ounce plastic bottles with instructions for dilution and use.  

B. **The Incident**  
On or about April 12, 2023, the Plaintiff, Sarah Mitchell, purchased a 16-ounce bottle of the “EcoClean Pro” multi-surface cleaner from a retail store located at 456 Main Street, Los Angeles, California. The bottle was unmarked except for the product label, which stated that the product was “non-toxic,” “eco-friendly,” and “safe for use on all surfaces.”  

On the same day, the Plaintiff used the product to clean a kitchen counter. After applying the cleaner to the surface, she left the area for approximately 15 minutes to attend to other tasks. Upon returning, she noticed a strong, pungent odor emanating from the surface. The surface appeared to be sticky and glistening, and upon closer inspection, the Plaintiff discovered that the cleaner had not been properly diluted, and instead had been applied undiluted.  

The Plaintiff attempted to clean the surface with a damp cloth and water, but the residue persisted, and it began to emit a strong, acrid smell that caused her to experience immediate irritation to her eyes, nose, and throat. She was unable to continue her normal activities and had to seek immediate medical attention at the Los Angeles Community Health Center.  


C. **Injuries Sustained**  
As a result of the exposure to the undiluted product, the Plaintiff suffered from a variety of injuries, including, but not limited to:  

1. **Respiratory Irritation:** The Plaintiff experienced severe coughing, difficulty breathing, and persistent wheezing.  
2. **Ocular Irritation:** The Plaintiff’s eyes became red, swollen, and extremely sensitive to light.  
3. **Dermal Irritation:** The Plaintiff suffered from a rash and irritation on her hands and arms, which persisted for several weeks.  
4. **Psychological Distress:** The Plaintiff experienced anxiety, panic attacks, and persistent fear of using similar products in the future.  
5. **Medical Expenses:** The Plaintiff incurred significant medical expenses, including diagnostic tests, treatment, and medications, as well as lost wages due to her inability to work during the recovery period.  

D. **Defective Nature of the Product**  
The Plaintiff’s injuries were directly caused by the defective nature of the product, which was not properly labeled, was not properly diluted, and was not safe for use as advertised. The product’s label did not contain any clear warnings or instructions regarding the necessity of dilution, and the packaging was misleading in its representation of the product’s safety and non-toxic nature.  

Furthermore, the Defendant’s marketing materials and promotional statements downplayed the potential hazards of using the product without proper dilution, which was a critical factor in the Plaintiff’s decision to use the product in its undiluted form.  

**V. NEGLIGENCE OF THE DEFENDANT**  


A. **Duty of Care**  
The Defendant, as a manufacturer and seller of consumer products, owed a duty of care to the Plaintiff and to all consumers who may use its products. This duty included, but was not limited to, the obligation to design, manufacture, and label products in a manner that is safe for their intended use and to warn consumers of potential risks and dangers associated with the product.  

B. **Breach of Duty**  
The Defendant breached its duty of care by:  

1. **Failing to Provide Adequate Warnings:** The product’s label failed to provide clear and conspicuous warnings regarding the necessity of dilution and the risks associated with using the product in its undiluted form.  
2. **Failing to Provide Proper Instructions:** The product’s instructions were incomplete and misleading, providing no clear guidance on how to dilute the product or how to use it safely.  
3. **Failing to Conduct Appropriate Testing:** The Defendant did not conduct adequate testing to determine the safety of the product in its undiluted form, and this failure was directly responsible for the Plaintiff’s injuries.  
4. **Misrepresenting Product Safety:** The Defendant misrepresented the product’s safety by promoting it as non-toxic and safe for use without dilution, which was not supported by the actual properties of the product.  

C. **Causation**  
The Defendant’s breach of its duty of care was a proximate cause of the Plaintiff’s injuries. The failure to warn and the failure to provide adequate instructions directly caused the Plaintiff to use the product in an unsafe manner, which in turn led to her exposure to the undiluted product and the subsequent injuries.  

D. **Damages**  
As a result of the Defendant’s negligence, the Plaintiff has suffered both economic and non-economic damages, including:  

1. **Medical Expenses:** The Plaintiff incurred significant medical expenses, including diagnostic testing, treatment, and medications.  
2. **Lost Wages:** The Plaintiff was unable to work for several weeks following the incident, resulting in a substantial loss of income.  
3. **Pain and Suffering:** The Plaintiff continues to suffer from physical and emotional pain, including chronic respiratory issues and anxiety related to the incident.  
4. **Loss of Enjoyment of Life:** The incident has significantly impacted the Plaintiff’s ability to engage in normal activities and has caused her to develop a lasting fear of using similar products in the future.  

**VI. STRICT LIABILITY IN PRODUCT LIABILITY**  

A. **Legal Standard**  
Under California law, a manufacturer, seller, or distributor may be held strictly liable for injuries caused by a defective product if the product was unreasonably dangerous and the defect existed at the time the product left the defendant’s control. This is a separate cause of action from negligence and is based on the concept of strict liability, which holds that a manufacturer is responsible for the safety of its products regardless of the level of care exercised in their production.  

B. **Defective Nature of the Product**  
The “EcoClean Pro” multi-surface cleaner was defective in its design and labeling. The product was not reasonably safe for its intended use, as it was not properly labeled with warnings or instructions regarding the necessity of dilution. The product was not designed to be used in an undiluted form, and its use in such a manner posed an unreasonable risk of harm to consumers.  

C. **Defect at the Time of Sale**  
The defect in the product existed at the time the product left the Defendant’s control. The Defendant failed to provide adequate warnings, instructions, and safety measures that would have made the product reasonably safe for its intended use. The defect was not the result of any misuse or alteration by the Plaintiff, but rather the result of the Defendant’s failure to ensure the product was safe and properly labeled.  

D. **Causation**  
The defective nature of the product was a direct and proximate cause of the Plaintiff’s injuries. The Plaintiff’s injuries were a foreseeable result of using the product in its undiluted form, which was not reasonably safe for its intended use. The Defendant’s failure to warn and to provide adequate instructions was a significant contributing factor to the Plaintiff’s harm.  

E. **Damages**  
The Plaintiff has suffered substantial damages as a result of the defective product, including medical expenses, lost wages, pain and suffering, and loss of enjoyment of life. These damages are directly attributable to the Defendant’s failure to ensure the product was safe and properly labeled.  

**VII. ALLEGATIONS AGAINST THE DEFENDANT**  

A. **Failure to Exercise Due Care in Product Design and Labeling**  
The Defendant failed to exercise due care in the design and labeling of the “EcoClean Pro” product. The product was not designed to be used in its undiluted form, and the labeling did not provide clear and sufficient warnings regarding the necessary dilution and the risks of using the product without dilution.  

B. **Failure to Warn of Known Dangers**  
The Defendant failed to provide adequate warnings of the dangers associated with using the product in its undiluted form. The product’s label did not include any warnings or instructions regarding the necessity of dilution, and this omission was a direct cause of the Plaintiff’s injuries.      

C. **Failure to Provide Adequate Instructions**  
The Defendant failed to provide clear and concise instructions for the safe use of the product. The instructions provided on the product were incomplete and misleading, leading the Plaintiff to use the product in an unsafe manner.  

D. **Marketing Practices and Misrepresentation**  
The Defendant engaged in deceptive marketing practices by promoting the product as non-toxic and safe for use without dilution. This misrepresentation was a direct cause of the Plaintiff’s decision to use the product in an unsafe manner.  

E. **Negligence in Manufacturing and Distribution**  
The Defendant failed to exercise the level of care required in the manufacturing and distribution of the product. The product was not tested adequately to determine its safety in its undiluted form, and this failure was directly responsible for the Plaintiff’s injuries.  

**VIII. DAMAGES SUSTAINED BY THE PLAINTIFF**  

A. **Economic Damages**  
The Plaintiff has incurred substantial economic damages as a result of the incident, including but not limited to:  

1. **Medical Expenses:** The Plaintiff has incurred medical expenses totaling approximately $12,500, including diagnostic tests, treatment, and medications.  
2. **Lost Wages:** The Plaintiff has lost wages amounting to approximately $8,000 due to her inability to work during the recovery period.  
3. **Future Medical Expenses:** The Plaintiff may require ongoing medical treatment for the injuries sustained, including therapy and follow-up visits, which could amount to additional costs.  

B. **Non-Economic Damages**  
The Plaintiff has also suffered significant non-economic damages, including:   


1. **Pain and Suffering:** The Plaintiff continues to suffer from physical and emotional pain as a result of the incident.  
2. **Loss of Enjoyment of Life:** The incident has significantly impacted the Plaintiff’s ability to engage in normal activities and has caused her to develop a lasting fear of using similar products in the future.  
3. **Psychological Distress:** The Plaintiff has suffered from anxiety and panic attacks as a result of the incident.  

**IX. CLAIMS FOR RELIEF**  

**COUNT I: NEGLIGENCE**  

1. The Defendant owed a duty of care to the Plaintiff.  
2. The Defendant breached that duty by failing to provide adequate warnings, instructions, and safety measures.  
3. The Defendant’s breach was the proximate cause of the Plaintiff’s injuries.  
4. The Plaintiff has suffered substantial damages as a result of the Defendant’s negligence.  
5. Therefore, the Defendant is liable for the Plaintiff’s injuries and damages.  

**COUNT II: STRICT LIABILITY IN PRODUCT LIABILITY**  

1. The product was defective in its design and labeling.  
2. The defect existed at the time the product left the Defendant’s control.  
3. The defect was a proximate cause of the Plaintiff’s injuries.  
4. The Plaintiff has suffered substantial damages as a result of the defective product.  
5. Therefore, the Defendant is strictly liable for the Plaintiff’s injuries and damages.  

**COUNT III: BREACH OF IMPLIED WARRANTY OF MERCHANTABILITY**  

1. The product was sold with an implied warranty of merchantability.  
2. The product was not fit for its intended use and was not reasonably safe for use.  
3. The Defendant failed to exercise due care in the sale and distribution of the product.  
4. The Plaintiff has suffered substantial damages as a result of the breach of this warranty.  
5. Therefore, the Defendant is liable for the Plaintiff’s injuries and damages.  

**COUNT IV: BREACH OF IMPLIED WARRANTY OF FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE**  

1. The product was sold for a specific purpose, namely, as a multi-surface cleaner.  
2. The product was not suitable for its intended purpose and was not reasonably safe for use.  
3. The Defendant failed to exercise due care in the sale and distribution of the product.  
4. The Plaintiff has suffered substantial damages as a result of the breach of this warranty.  
5. Therefore, the Defendant is liable for the Plaintiff’s injuries and damages.  

**X. PRAYER FOR RELIEF**  

WHEREFORE, Plaintiff, Sarah Mitchell, respectfully prays that this Court enter judgment in her favor and against the Defendant, EcoSafe Manufacturing, Inc., and award the following relief:  

A. **Compensatory Damages:** Including, but not limited to, medical expenses, lost wages, and future medical expenses, as well as pain and suffering, loss of enjoyment of life, and psychological distress.  

B. **Punitive Damages:** In an amount to be determined by the Court, as the Defendant’s conduct was reckless, grossly negligent, and in violation of the standards of care required of a responsible manufacturer.  

C. **Attorney’s Fees and Costs:** The Plaintiff is entitled to recover the reasonable attorney’s fees and costs incurred in this action, pursuant to applicable law.  

  D. **Injunctive Relief:** The Plaintiff requests that the Court issue an injunction prohibiting the Defendant from selling or distributing the “EcoClean Pro” product in its current form until such time as it has been redesigned, properly labeled, and adequately tested for safety.  

E. **Other Relief:** Any and all other relief that the Court deems just and equitable under the circumstances.  

**XI. JURY TRIAL DEMAND**  

Plaintiff hereby demands a trial by jury on all issues so triable by jury as provided by law.  

**XII. CERTIFICATE OF SERVICE**  

Plaintiff hereby certifies that this Complaint was served on the Defendant, EcoSafe Manufacturing, Inc., on April 20, 2024, via certified mail, return receipt requested, and that the Defendant was served with a copy of this Complaint and all exhibits attached thereto.      

**XIII. EXHIBITS**  

A. **Exhibit A:** Photographs of the product label and packaging.  
B. **Exhibit B:** Medical records and treatment documentation.  
C. **Exhibit C:** Invoice for medical expenses.  
D. **Exhibit D:** Receipt for the purchase of the product.      

E. **Exhibit E:** Statements from the Plaintiff and witnesses.  
F. **Exhibit F:** Product specifications and safety data sheets.  

G. **Exhibit G:** Marketing materials and promotional statements.  
H. **Exhibit H:** Expert opinion and testimony regarding the safety of the product.  

**XIV. ADDITIONAL CLAIMS**  

A. **Unjust Enrichment:** The Defendant has unjustly benefited from the sale of the defective product, and the Plaintiff is entitled to recover the value of the product, as well as the damages sustained as a result of its use.  

B. **Consumer Fraud:** The Defendant engaged in deceptive and fraudulent practices by misrepresenting the safety of the product and by failing to provide adequate warnings and instructions.  

C. **Consumer Protection Act Violations:** The Defendant’s actions constitute a violation of California’s Consumer Legal Remedies Act, as the product was sold in a manner that was deceptive and unfair to consumers.  

D. **Product Recall:** The Defendant has failed to initiate a product recall or to notify consumers of the dangers associated with the product, thereby exposing consumers to unnecessary risks.  

**XV. CONCLUSION**  

Plaintiff respectfully submits that this Complaint presents a clear and compelling case for the relief sought, and that the Defendant’s conduct has caused substantial harm to the Plaintiff and other consumers who have used the product. This action is brought to ensure that the Defendant is held accountable for its negligence, and to prevent further harm to consumers in the future.

**XVI. DEFINITIONS AND INTERPRETATION**  

For the purpose of this Complaint, the following terms shall have the meanings set forth below:  

A. **“Product”** means the “EcoClean Pro” multi-surface cleaner, including all related components, packaging, labels, and instructions provided by the Defendant at the time of sale.  

B. **“Defect”** includes, but is not limited to, any failure in design, manufacturing, or labeling of the Product, which renders the Product unreasonably dangerous for its intended use.  

C. **“Intended Use”** means the use of the Product as described in the labeling, advertising, and marketing materials provided by the Defendant.  

D. **“Consumer”** refers to any individual who purchased or used the Product, including the Plaintiff, Sarah Mitchell.  

E. **“Manufacturer”** refers to the Defendant, EcoSafe Manufacturing, Inc., or any other entity that designed, produced, or distributed the Product.  

F. **“Warranty”** includes both express and implied warranties, including the implied warranty of merchantability and the implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose.  

G. **“Causation”** means the legal relationship between the Defendant’s conduct and the Plaintiff’s injuries, which must be proximate and foreseeable.  

H. **“Duty”** means the legal obligation of the Defendant to exercise reasonable care in the design, manufacturing, labeling, and distribution of the Product.  

I. **“Negligence”** refers to the failure of the Defendant to exercise the level of care that a reasonably prudent manufacturer would exercise under similar circumstances.  

J. **“Strict Liability”** refers to the legal principle that holds a manufacturer responsible for injuries caused by a defective product, regardless of the level of care exercised in its production or distribution.  

K. **“Misrepresentation”** means any false or misleading statement made by the Defendant regarding the safety, efficacy, or proper use of the Product.  

L. **“Product Recall”** refers to the Defendant’s failure to notify consumers of the dangers associated with the Product or to take corrective action to prevent further harm.  

M. **“Consequential Damages”** includes any damages that are not directly caused by the Product but are a result of the Plaintiff’s injuries, including lost wages, diminished earning capacity, and psychological harm.  

N. **“Unjust Enrichment”** refers to the unjust benefit that the Defendant has derived from the sale of the Product, which is directly linked to the harm suffered by the Plaintiff.  

O. **“Consumer Legal Remedies Act”** refers to the California Business and Professions Code, which governs the rights of consumers against unfair and deceptive business practices.  

P. **“Statute of Limitations”** refers to the period within which a claim may be brought against the Defendant, which in this case has not expired due to the discovery rule.  

**XVII. REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES**  

A. **Representation of Product Safety**  
The Defendant represented to the Plaintiff and to all consumers that the Product was safe for use as described in the labeling and marketing materials. This representation was false and misleading, as the Product was not reasonably safe for its intended use when used in an undiluted form.  

B. **Representation of Compliance with Laws**  
The Defendant represented that the Product complied with all applicable laws and regulations governing consumer products. This representation was false, as the Product failed to meet the safety standards required by California law and other applicable consumer protection statutes.

The Defendant represented that the Product was of high quality and suitable for its intended use. This representation was false, as the Product was defective in its design and labeling, and it failed to meet the quality standards expected by consumers.

The Defendant represented that the Product would perform as described in the labeling and marketing materials. This representation was false, as the Product did not perform as intended when used in an undiluted form, leading to the Plaintiff’s injuries.

The Defendant represented that the Product was not defective and was free from defects that could cause harm to consumers. This representation was false, as the Product was defective in its design and labeling, and it was not fit for its intended use.

The Defendant represented that the Product was properly labeled with all necessary warnings and instructions. This representation was false, as the Product was not properly labeled and failed to include clear warnings regarding the necessity of dilution and the risks associated with using the Product in its undiluted form.
